modelDescription: An OC3-level threat actor (~10 individuals, ~$1m resources) operates as a ransomware affiliate or independent operator, specialising in exploiting vulnerabilities in public-facing web applications to target large enterprises with revenue between $250m and $1bn. The actor conducts double extortion — both encrypting data and exfiltrating it — and targets organisations at Cybersecurity Capability Maturity Model level MIL2/MIL3, seeking median ransom payouts on the order of $1m.

Cybench: T1486, T1490, T1489 - Data Encryption & System Disruption

scenarioInputs.heading: Type of Actor
scenarioInputs.content:
* We consider a threat actor that would broadly be classified as a small cybercrime syndicate having OC3 level operational capacity (~10 individuals, with resources of ~$1m, and loosely based on the corresponding threat actor definition provided in [RAND](https://www.rand.org/pubs/research_reports/RRA2849-1.html)). This threat actor is interested in what is sometimes referred to as 'big-game hunting'.
* The threat actor is assumed to operate either as a RaaS affiliate (per definitions in [NCSC](https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/whitepaper/ransomware-extortion-and-the-cyber-crime-ecosystem)), or may have developed its own ransomware, or modified someone else's. Either way, we assume that the threat actor primarily makes its money through cyber attacks (as opposed to selling its services to other criminals) and specialises in achieving initial access by exploiting vulnerabilities in public-facing web apps and devices.
* If operating as a RaaS affiliate, the threat actor is expected to bring their own customisations to the task of deploying the RaaS operator's capabilities, and has the capability to adaptively make use of tools, and 'living off the land' approaches in moving through the attacked network (per [CISA provided information on LockBit affiliates](https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa23-165a)).
* The threat actor is assumed to have pre-existing cyberattack infrastructure, but no pre-existing access to the attacked organisation.
* In our scenario, we envisage some division of labour, with different members of the cybercrime syndicate specialising in different areas, such as malware customisation/development, infrastructure operation/acquisition, network penetration, negotiation/coercion, crypto-laundering etc. It should be noted that the RaaS operator may perform many of these activities on behalf of the affiliate, and also that it is commonplace for the affiliate to just provide the initial access, conduct the attack, exfiltrate the data and leave everything else including malware development, negotiation and payment, crypto-laundering etc to the RaaS operator. (Appendix C - contains more info on ransomware crime syndicate group size).

scenarioInputs.heading: Type of Target
scenarioInputs.content:
* The threat actor targets large enterprises, hoping to realise a median ransom payout of somewhere on the order of $1m.
* Approximate company sizes of interest in our scenario are:
  * Revenue $250m to $1bn
    * Such companies can command $1m-$2m payouts (see [Sophos (2025)](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware))
  * Assume many hundreds or a few thousand employees
    * Average UK company has an annual revenue per employee of £118k (=$162k)
    * Though average revenue per employee can vary greatly between industries (see [CompanySights](https://www.companysights.com/resources/revenue-per-employee-a-key-benchmarking-metric-for-businesses)). According to Companysights the average revenue per employee is between $200k and $500k.
    * Taking the CompanySights figures…
      * A $250m revenue company might be expected to have 500 to 1250 employees
      * A $1bn revenue company might be expected to have 2000 to 5000 employees
* The threat actor especially targets companies relying on IT systems for operations, e.g. logistics, manufacturing, retail, such that encryption ransomware, and its associated loss of availability for the target has significant impact (in addition to the impact of confidentiality loss associated with data exfiltration).

scenarioInputs.heading: Type of Vector
scenarioInputs.content:
* From Chart 1, [Sophos (2025)](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware) it can be seen that a range of initial access vectors are used for ransomware and 'exploited vulnerabilities' is on average the most common over the last 3 years, ranging from 28% up to 40% of initial accesses (40% for businesses in the 500-1000 employee bracket).
* Note that info from [Coveware](https://www.coveware.com/blog/2024/1/25/new-ransomware-reporting-requirements-kick-in-as-victims-increasingly-avoid-paying?utm_source=chatgpt.com) suggests that CVEs are also often favoured by actors going after bigger enterprise targets
* [NCSC](https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/whitepaper/ransomware-extortion-and-the-cyber-crime-ecosystem) also identified 'direct exploitation' as a prominent initial access vector for ransomware
  * ".... many [criminals]…conduct the scanning themselves…. Criminals look for devices that are likely to be in businesses (rather than home environments). Examples include Microsoft Exchange servers, platforms such as Citrix or VMware, VPN devices and firewall devices".
  * "Criminal use of exploits often surges shortly after certain critical patches are released indicating they are being reverse engineered from the patches. In most cases, an exploit is widely available in the criminal forums in less than one week from the patch being released. A zero-day exploit is a recently discovered vulnerability, not yet known to vendors or antivirus companies, that criminals can exploit. Cyber criminals don't need to develop their own zero-day exploits as doing so is expensive, and there are many devices 'in the wild' that are not patched regularly. However, some actors have been known to use zero-day exploits, most notably there are public reports of Cl0p's use of the Accellion, GoAnywhere and MOVEit vulnerabilities".
* Example type of vector
  * According to [RecordedFuture](https://www.recordedfuture.com/research/patterns-targets-ransomware-exploitation-vulnerabilities-2017-2023), certain classes of threat actors most favour vulnerabilities that have high impact in terms of access or control over systems and the ubiquity of the affected software. They also state "... we also found that when many ransomware groups target a vulnerability, they do so almost always because it can be exploited with minimal lines of malicious code that can be easily implemented into mass scanning activity (through malicious HTTP requests, for example)"
  * Some example vulnerabilities that that LockBit affiliates have used, according to [CISA](https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa23-165a) include:
    * [CVE-2019-0708](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2019-0708): Microsoft Remote Desktop Services Remote Code Execution Vulnerability
    * [CVE-2018-13379](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2018-13379): Fortinet FortiOS Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) Virtual Private Network (VPN) Path Traversal Vulnerability.

scenarioInputs.heading: Defense Level
scenarioInputs.content: * Assume cybersecurity maturity level MIL2 (see [Cybersecurity Capability Maturity Model](https://www.energy.gov/ceser/cybersecurity-capability-maturity-model-c2m2), CCMM) across all maturity domains with MIL3 in some domains.

scenarioInputs.heading: Intent
scenarioInputs.content:
* The attacker's intent is financial monetization through ransom.
* Specifically we assume that the attacker uses double extortion. This comprises both data encryption ('availability' impact) as well as data exfiltration ('confidentiality' impact). Double extortion is a more complex attack, but can command higher reward than data exfiltration alone. The threat actor demands payment of a ransom in exchange for provision of decryption keys and a promise not to publicise exfiltrated data.
* The greater levels of threat actor skill and capability required to conduct extortion via encryption of systems, over and above the skill required for just data exfiltration extortion, distinguishes this double extortion approach as something that an actor at RAND operational Capability OC3 might be more inclined to do, or be better able to do, than an actor at operational capability level OC2.
* [Coveware](https://www.coveware.com/blog/2023/7/21/ransom-monetization-rates-fall-to-record-low-despite-jump-in-average-ransom-payments?utm_source=chatgpt.com) provides support for the above, indicating how attacks such as double extortion can achieve bigger returns, but require more work on behalf of the threat actor, and the broad skills required may mean that multiple individuals often need to work together in some form of syndicate.

attackSteps.description: The affiliate performs active scanning of internet-facing assets for known vulnerabilities. Some basic OSINT will almost certainly be used to size up victims (e.g. revenue, insurance) to set ransom demands
attackSteps.failureMode: If recon fails (e.g. no vulnerabilities or intel found), the attack cannot even begin.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Active scanning is LLM relevant, must-have. Probability of success is very high (~100%) - so we include the tactic, but do not break it down to technique level for further analysis

attackSteps.description: The affiliate must obtain or prepare tools, malware, and infrastructure to conduct the attack. In the RaaS model, some resources come ready-made (e.g. the ransomware payload and a leak site for publishing stolen data are provided by the RaaS service).
attackSteps.failureMode: If resource prep fails (e.g. no exploit, no malware or infrastructure), the operation stalls – the affiliate lacks tools or access to proceed.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Resource development is essential and at least one aspect could be LLM relevant: Obtaining exploit proofs/code (T1588.005). However, probability of success is assumed to be 100% so we stay at tactic level. Essentially, if the attacker does not have the necessary resources then the attack cannot commence (and hence any such absence of resource is effectively accounted for in the number of attempts / actor / year metric)
techniques: T1583.006 – Acquire Infrastructure: Virtual Private Server

attackSteps.description: In our scenario, the group focuses on vulnerability exploits against Internet-facing systems.
attackSteps.failureMode: If initial access fails (no foothold gained), the affiliate cannot penetrate the victim's network – the ransomware deployment is a non-starter.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Must have. Only one technique, so stay at tactic level

attackSteps.description: After initial access, the affiliate needs to launch tools: e.g. starting a remote shell, running malware. Ransomware operators often use scripts and admin tools for execution
attackSteps.failureMode: If execution fails (malicious code never runs), the attacker's payloads and commands don't take effect and the attack fails.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Must have, but keep at tactic level since though there are alternatives, none seem particularly LLM relevant

attackSteps.description: Persistence involves maintaining long-term access to systems (surviving reboots, credential changes, etc.). Ransomware affiliates do often implement persistence – for example, adding new accounts – but it's not strictly required if they can complete their attack quickly. [Proofpoint](https://www.proofpoint.com/us/blog/email-and-cloud-threats/eight-stages-of-the-ransomware-attack-chain#:~:text=In%202022%2C%20Coveware%20reported%20that,won%20access) reports that in 2022, 82% of observed ransomware attacks included some form of persistence tactic. However, dwell times are reducing and persistence could be optional. Hence we do not include it.

Ransomware affiliates almost always seek higher privileges after initial entry. Administrative or root access is needed to spread ransomware broadly and to turn off defenses or backups, achieving domain admin on the network is particularly valuable. If this step fails, the adversary's reach is severely limited. With only user-level access, they might not get access to critical servers or data and might be unable to propagate the ransomware across the network.
attackSteps.failureMode: If privilege escalation fails, the attackers are stuck with low-level rights. They may be unable to disable security tools or access sensitive data, often stopping the attack from progressing beyond the initial host.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Stay at tactic level, since it's unclear that there is much to be gained by way of LLM estimatability in breaking down to technique level.

attackSteps.description: Defense Evasion is critical when attacking a well-defended large enterprise. However, we assume that this tactic is already accounted for when we consider probability of success of other tactics. Hence we do not include it, to avoid double-counting.

Whilst credential access is likely to be a common tactic in ransomware operations (such as use of LSASS dumping and Mimikatz), we are already accounting for something similar with the 'privilege escalation' tactic and the associated 'valid accounts' technique. To avoid double counting, and because it is a 'nice to have', we therefore do not include the credential access tactic.

Once inside, affiliates perform Discovery to understand the environment. This reconnaissance phase inside the network lets them locate where valuable data resides (file servers, databases) and identify defenses or backup systems to circumvent
attackSteps.failureMode: If internal discovery fails, the attackers may miss critical systems or data. They could encrypt some machines blindly, but might overlook backups or high-value servers, reducing the impact. Lack of knowledge can also lead to mistakes (e.g. tripping alarms) or an incomplete data theft, weakening their leverage.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Uncertainty is large regarding how an actor actually proceeds, since there are many potentially applicable techniques. So will stay at the tactic level to avoid overfitting or adding speculative details.

attackSteps.description: Lateral Movement is how the attacker pivots from the initial foothold to broader control of the network. Ransomware affiliates aim to infect as many machines and access as much data as possible. They use techniques like connecting to other systems with stolen credentials or deploying remote execution tools.
attackSteps.failureMode: If lateral movement fails, the compromise remains limited to the initial host or a small subset of systems. The affiliate then might only encrypt a trivial portion of the network or grab a small amount of data, which may not be enough pressure to make the victim pay.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Not clear if there is benefit in breaking into technique level, for purposes of determining LLM uplift. For techniques like Lateral Tool Transfer, and use of Remote Services, an LLM would likely do about as well as a human, since these techniques usually involve pretty straightforward command execution. However, if LLMs enable new exploits to be developed in Remote Services, then that could potentially be a source of uplift. There is also uncertainty, about which of these techniques an actor is most likely to use or have available, making estimation complex. So stay at tactic level.

attackSteps.description: In the context of double extortion, Collection of victim data is a must. Before launching ransomware, affiliates quietly search for and aggregate valuable files: databases, client records, intellectual property, emails – anything that would hurt to see leaked publicly.
attackSteps.failureMode: If collection fails (attackers can't gather any sensitive data), the 'double' in double-extortion is lost. The attackers would have no files to leak, weakening their bargaining position – they'd be left with just encryption, which many well-prepared victims can recover from.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Not clear there is much, if anything, in the way of LLM uplift here, so could stick at tactic level.

attackSteps.description: Command and Control (C2) is the adversary's lifeline into the victim environment. After establishing a foothold, affiliates need a communication channel to send commands, coordinate lateral movement, and extract data. Ransomware operators often repurpose trusted channels: for instance, using standard protocols (HTTP/HTTPS, DNS) or legitimate remote access software to blend in.
attackSteps.failureMode: If C2 fails (the affiliate loses contact with their malware/beacons inside the network), they can't direct the attack. The team would effectively be 'locked out' from interacting with compromised systems, stopping further progression like escalation, lateral moves, exfiltration, etc.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Not clear there is much scope for using LLMs here, though potentially they could help with making transfers more stealthy. More importantly, it's not clear that there will be much difference in the potential benefits of LLMs depending on the specifics of the communication channels used. Stay at tactic level.

attackSteps.description: Exfiltration is the act of stealing the collected data out to attacker-controlled infrastructure. Double-extortion schemes hinge on this.
attackSteps.failureMode: If exfiltration fails (attackers cannot transmit the stolen data out of the victim's network), the affiliate loses the data-leverage part of the extortion.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: As for command and control, it's not clear there's much scope for using LLMs here, and even if there is, it's not clear that there will be much difference in their potential benefits depending on the specifics of the exfiltration mechanism. So stay at tactic level.

attackSteps.description: The Impact phase is where the attackers encrypt data on all reachable systems. This renders the victim organization's files and services unusable, causing operational paralysis. Affiliates also take additional impact actions: they often inhibit system recovery by deleting backups and shadow volume copies. Some ransomware even changes wallpapers or drops ransom notes in directories to ensure the victim knows they've been hit.
attackSteps.failureMode: If impact fails (e.g. files don't get encrypted or critical services remain unaffected), the victim's operations continue relatively unscathed. With no significant disruption or data loss, the victim has little incentive to pay, and the affiliate's attack effectively fails to achieve its goal.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: It's not clear there's much scope for using LLMs for the encryption and data destruction related techniques (T1486, T1490, T1489, T1485). However, LLMs might be used to assist with the social engineering (T1657) related to the extortion negotiations. Hence we separate out this technique.

nodes.name: Reconnaissance
nodes.baselineRationale: Adversaries commonly discover exposed systems and known vulnerabilities via active scanning, which is almost guaranteed to succeed if any services are exposed. The affiliate performs active scanning of internet-facing assets for known vulnerabilities, and some basic OSINT will almost certainly be used to size up victims (e.g. revenue, insurance) to set ransom demands.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Resource Development
nodes.baselineRationale: Ransomware affiliates will almost certainly gather the needed exploits and malware before attacking. This includes purchasing exploit code for known CVEs and RaaS payloads, renting servers, etc. Defenders cannot directly interfere at this stage. Success is effectively assured.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Initial Access
nodes.baselineRationale: Here we assume that, through reconnaissance (active scanning) the threat actor has already found an exposed vulnerable port, so probabilities are conditioned on this fact. Key factors that might stop the attack include Web Application Firewalls and intrusion detection and response, though a WAF or EDR may not block an attack and rather may just raise alerts. Exploits might be developed to avoid WAF rules, and attackers may target security devices which might not have intrusion monitoring yet. [Edgescan](https://www.edgescan.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/2024-Vulnerability-Statistics-Report.pdf) (2023) provide detailed analysis of CVEs but do not give figures for the likelihood of success of exploits. Attackers will naturally select the particular vulnerabilities and associated exploits which have high probabilities of success and which yield good options for onward privilege escalation, lateral movement, etc. According to [Qualys (2023)](https://blog.qualys.com/vulnerabilities-threat-research/2023/12/19/2023-threat-landscape-year-in-review-part-one), 206 vulnerabilities had weaponised exploit code available and exploits for these vulnerabilities are highly likely to compromise the target system if used. The same values as for the SME case are assumed.
nodes.sotaRationale: An LLM at this capability level (basic to moderate web vulnerability exploitation) provides meaningful but limited enhancement to OC3 actors conducting initial access via n-day exploits. The LLM excels at accelerating exploit adaptation, troubleshooting, and parameter guidance — tasks where known vulnerability patterns apply. However, the capability ceiling constrains impact against MIL2/MIL3 defences: the LLM cannot bypass sophisticated WAFs, IDS systems, or perform complex target-specific adaptations that would significantly improve success rates. OC3 actors already possess core exploitation knowledge, so the LLM primarily assists with automation and basic scripting rather than enabling fundamentally new capabilities. The improvement translates to a modest uplift over the baseline, with the primary value being operational efficiency gains for junior operators and faster iteration on exploit variants.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM capable of solving expert-level exploitation tasks (such as complex deserialization vulnerabilities) provides more substantial assistance for initial access, particularly through sophisticated payload adaptation, WAF/IDS evasion technique generation, and rapid troubleshooting. The LLM can generate bypass variations, assist with encoding and obfuscation, and provide expert-level guidance on exploit customisation. However, the baseline already reflects strong OC3 actor capabilities, so the improvement represents optimisation rather than enablement. Operational factors such as target selection, timing, and stealth still depend on human judgement. The wide uncertainty range reflects variability in how effectively these capabilities transfer from structured benchmark tasks to real-world exploitation scenarios against defended enterprises.

nodes.name: Execution
nodes.baselineRationale: Execution (i.e. adversary-controlled code running on victim systems) will have to be successfully performed multiple times on multiple machines as the attacker moves laterally and seeks to encrypt data on different systems. Ransomware affiliates commonly use PowerShell, script interpreters, or built-in tools for execution. Large enterprises invest in endpoint security solutions like anti-virus and EDR, and many enable features like script block logging, constrained PowerShell, or application whitelisting on servers. However, ransomware affiliates use stealthy techniques: they may use signed or built-in tools (living off the land) to avoid detection, run malicious code via memory injection, or use admin tools (WMIC, scheduled tasks) that EDR might not flag immediately. It is also possible that if one execution technique fails an attacker could try another, and it is known for a single affiliate to use multiple RaaS strains. The same figures as for the SME case are assumed.
nodes.sotaRationale: An LLM at this capability level demonstrates basic vulnerability research capability, which has moderate positive correlation with execution tradecraft but significant domain mismatch. Against a MIL2/MIL3 enterprise with EDR and SOC, the LLM provides modest tactical assistance — primarily through script generation, obfuscation suggestions, command reference, and technique variety. However, the critical challenge of EDR evasion requires adaptive, context-specific operational tradecraft that exceeds this LLM's capability ceiling. OC3 actors are already sophisticated, so the LLM cannot meaningfully enhance their ability to adapt to modern behavioural defences. The improvement is constrained by EDR sophistication and the LLM's capability ceiling being below advanced evasion techniques, resulting in marginal efficiency gains rather than breakthrough capability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM capable of expert-level exploit development demonstrates advanced technical capabilities that translate meaningfully to the execution phase through accelerated variant generation, broadened technique selection, and superior payload generation and obfuscation. The LLM can assist with sophisticated evasion technique combinations and deeper understanding of defensive mechanisms. However, OC3 actors already possess significant execution expertise, and success remains heavily dependent on environmental factors — EDR configuration, SOC capabilities, and network architecture — that the LLM cannot control. Critical operational aspects including OPSEC, timing, and detection response remain human-dependent. The improvement reflects that technical components represent only part of execution success, with the net effect being a moderate uplift tempered by the operational nature of the task.

nodes.name: Privilege Escalation
nodes.baselineRationale: Relevant techniques include T1068 (Exploitation for Privilege Escalation) using exploits like ZeroLogon and PrintNightmare, or T1003 (OS Credential Dumping). Large organisations typically maintain a tiered admin model — compromise of a low-level user does not immediately yield Domain Admin. Many require MFA for key admin actions or remote access, which can thwart use of stolen credentials alone. Most known privilege escalation vulnerabilities have been patched or mitigated in up-to-date enterprises. However, attackers often find that some credentials provide elevated access (e.g. admin password reuse, overlooked service accounts). An enterprise SOC might detect abnormal privilege escalation, though in larger organisations even those alert types can be prone to false positives. According to [JumpCloud](https://jumpcloud.com/blog/multi-factor-authentication-statistics), MFA usage is 34% at medium-sized companies versus 87% at large enterprises. The same figures as for the SME case are assumed.
nodes.sotaRationale: An LLM at this capability level provides marginal assistance to already-skilled OC3 actors, primarily through enumeration scripting, technique catalogue access, and systematic coverage of potential escalation vectors. Knowledge is not the bottleneck — OC3 actors already know standard escalation techniques — and detection remains the limiting factor, which this LLM capability level cannot address. The improvement reflects efficiency gains rather than new capabilities, with the LLM helping to ensure more thorough coverage of credential stores, misconfigurations, and known escalation paths. The wide uncertainty range reflects both baseline defence variability and uncertain LLM impact magnitude, with real-world constraints on integration adding friction.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM capable of expert-level vulnerability research and exploit construction provides meaningful but not transformative value for privilege escalation. The primary benefit comes from comprehensive technique enumeration, optimised credential dumping, CVE exploitation guidance, and troubleshooting support. The LLM can help navigate defensive controls and identify alternative escalation paths more quickly. However, the baseline indicates already-sophisticated actors, limiting the improvement potential. Operational execution, real-time defensive responses, and fundamental security architecture constraints cap the impact. The domain transfer from web application security to enterprise OS/network security introduces additional uncertainty about how effectively these capabilities translate.

nodes.name: Discovery
nodes.baselineRationale: The attacker needs to find key servers (AD, file servers, backups). Large networks make this harder due to size and network segmentation, but also easier to hide in (given the volume of legitimate traffic). If the attacker obtains admin credentials, they can use standard IT tools (AD search, network scanners running as admin) which might not immediately set off alarms if done stealthily. It is common to use 'living off the land' tools for later TTPs. [Barracuda XDR](https://cybersecurityasia.net/ransomware-stopped-during-lateral-movement/) reports that 48% of their 1H2024 security alerts were for network reconnaissance. [Expert Insights](https://expertinsights.com/endpoint-security/edr-market-overview) (2024) suggests ~50% of companies had XDR, and 50% had AV on endpoints. There is a reasonably high likelihood that the attacker can perform necessary discovery operations, particularly if privilege escalation to admin has been achieved. The same figures as for the SME case are assumed.
nodes.sotaRationale: An LLM at this capability level provides only marginal assistance for discovery. The baseline success rate of 85% reflects that OC3 actors already possess strong knowledge and tools for network enumeration with admin rights. The LLM can offer modest help through script automation, systematic enumeration checklists, and reduced operator errors, but it cannot meaningfully address the primary failure mode — SOC detection — which accounts for the majority of baseline failures. The different problem domain (post-compromise enumeration versus vulnerability analysis) further limits the LLM's applicability, and the high baseline creates a ceiling effect that constrains improvement potential regardless of tool sophistication.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM at expert-level capability far exceeds the difficulty of standard post-exploitation enumeration tasks. However, the baseline success rate is already very high because standard reconnaissance tools with admin rights are highly effective. The LLM provides incremental value through better command optimisation, faster data parsing, more comprehensive enumeration strategies, and improved edge case handling. The primary constraints on discovery success — network segmentation and SOC monitoring — are operational factors that the LLM cannot fundamentally alter. The improvement is modest, reflecting that OC3 actors are already proficient at this core ransomware tradecraft, and the domain shift from application security to system administration limits the direct transferability of the LLM's demonstrated capabilities.

nodes.name: Lateral Movement
nodes.baselineRationale: Attackers might have to exploit remote services (T1210), taking advantage of vulnerabilities in internal services. Large networks may have legacy apps that could be targeted. Many organisations run internal intrusion prevention that might catch such exploits or unusual lateral traffic. A SOC might notice if an employee account suddenly tries RDP into multiple servers. [Barracuda XDR](https://cybersecurityasia.net/ransomware-stopped-during-lateral-movement/) indicates that lateral movement is the clearest sign of ransomware activity, with 44% of ransomware attacks spotted by the lateral movement detection engine. [Sophos (2024)](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware) states that large organisations often have a somewhat larger proportion of computers infected (~48%) than smaller organisations (e.g. 39% for $10m-$50m revenue), possibly due to increased centralised management of devices. Large enterprises are more likely to have backups stored in isolated locations. According to [Veeam (2021)](https://www.veeam.com/company/press-release/cxo-research-58-percent-of-data-backups-are-failing-creating-data-protection-challenges-and-limiting-digital-transformation-initiatives.html), 58% of backups fail during recovery. Figures are somewhat reduced over those used in the SME case.
nodes.sotaRationale: An LLM at this capability level provides meaningful but limited assistance with lateral movement. It can help with technical execution — scripting credential extraction, understanding protocols, generating obfuscated commands — yielding a modest improvement over the baseline. However, the core challenges of lateral movement in MIL2/MIL3 environments — evading behavioural EDR detection, navigating network segmentation, and making real-time OPSEC decisions — are beyond the LLM's demonstrated capability. The benchmark demonstrates static code analysis, while lateral movement requires real-time operational execution and adaptive evasion. OC3 actors already possess sophisticated lateral movement capabilities, so the LLM's primary value is reducing operational friction (faster iteration, fewer errors) rather than overcoming the main obstacles.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM at expert-level capability demonstrates strong multi-step reasoning and technical knowledge integration, providing meaningful assistance for lateral movement through comprehensive technique and tool guidance, OPSEC best practices, and strategic planning optimisation. The LLM can assist with credential extraction and parsing, living-off-the-land technique selection, and automation of lateral movement scripting. However, there is significant domain divergence between code exploitation and infrastructure-level operations, and the MIL2/MIL3 defensive environment (SOC, EDR, network segmentation) presents challenges that the LLM cannot directly overcome. Lateral movement success fundamentally depends on operational tradecraft, real-time environmental adaptation, and stealth — capabilities where the LLM provides incremental rather than transformative assistance.

nodes.name: Collection
nodes.baselineRationale: In double extortion, attackers seek the most sensitive data. Large enterprises often silo data — e.g. HR records, R&D files — each with restricted access. If the attacker has Domain Admin privileges, they can often bypass many restrictions (by changing permissions or impersonating users). Some sensitive databases might use encryption or separate admin roles that even Domain Admin cannot directly read. If the company has DLP, large file transfers or suspicious access patterns could be blocked or flagged. According to [Resilience Forward (2024)](https://resilienceforward.com/data-protection-survey-sees-a-large-year-on-year-increase-in-data-encryption-usage/), almost half (46%) of organisations now require all data to be encrypted as standard, double the percentage from 2023. [GlobeNewsWire (2024)](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2024/02/29/2838130/0/en/87-of-Companies-to-Increase-Investment-in-Encryption-Technologies-in-2024.html) reports that 71% of organisations have adopted a formal encryption programme. The same figures as for the SME case are assumed.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Command and Control
nodes.baselineRationale: Most large companies allow web traffic (80/443) from user networks, so attackers use HTTPS to an innocuous-looking endpoint (or a cloud app). They may also piggyback on existing remote access tools or VPNs — some affiliates use RMM tools to maintain access. The SOC of a large enterprise might spot unusual traffic (including DNS tunnelling, strange hours of communication), but not always in time. Many defenders focus on blocking known bad addresses, but a new custom C2 channel can slip by initially. Large organisations impose more hurdles for C2, but a prepared actor will usually circumvent them at least long enough to accomplish objectives. The same probabilities of success as for the SME case are assumed.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Exfiltration
nodes.baselineRationale: A large company may implement DLP (Data Loss Prevention), egress controls, and simple threshold rules monitoring network traffic or even detection via usage/bills. History shows even large companies frequently suffer data leaks in ransomware events, indicating that fully blocking exfiltration is difficult. Many large enterprises deploy DLP solutions at the network boundary that can detect or block known sensitive data patterns leaving. They may restrict use of external cloud storage or web upload services from corporate networks. Large outbound transfers might trigger alarms or throttling. Some organisations inspect SSL traffic for known file movement (though this is challenging). The same probability of success as for the SME case is assumed.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: T1486, T1490, T1489 - Data Encryption & System Disruption
nodes.baselineRationale: In the final stage, the affiliate deploys the ransomware payload to encrypt data and disrupt operations, and they try to destroy backups to prevent recovery. Large enterprises can be expected to maintain backups. According to [Veeam (2021)](https://www.veeam.com/company/press-release/cxo-research-58-percent-of-data-backups-are-failing-creating-data-protection-challenges-and-limiting-digital-transformation-initiatives.html), in 2021 58% of backups failed during recovery due to factors like outdated technology, inadequate testing, and malware infection. [Sophos (2025)](https://assets.sophos.com/X24WTUEQ/at/9brgj5n44hqvgsp5f5bqcps/sophos-state-of-ransomware-2024-wp.pdf) reports 57% of backup compromise attempts were successful. [Sophos (2025)](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware) states that in 2025, in 50% of attacks data was encrypted, in 44% the attack was stopped before encryption, and in 6% data was not encrypted but the victim was still held to ransom. The largest organisations surveyed were most likely to have data encrypted, with 65% of attacks on organisations with 3,001-5,000 employees resulting in data encryption. By the time the attacker has got this far, they will likely have established significant privileges and control. The same figures as for the SME case are used.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM provides marginal assistance at the impact stage, primarily through improved script generation, documentation lookup, systematic backup enumeration, and troubleshooting. However, the primary failure modes at this stage — EDR detection and SOC response — are beyond the LLM's demonstrated capability ceiling. The OC3 actors are already sophisticated with established playbooks and the high baseline success rate indicates strong existing competence, leaving limited room for improvement. The LLM's capability at solving structured technical challenges has moderate but not strong relevance to ransomware deployment, which is more operationally focused than algorithmically complex. The wide uncertainty reflects compounded uncertainty in both the baseline and the LLM's ability to meaningfully address the remaining failure modes.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM's demonstrated expert-level technical capability indicates strong reasoning but limited transferability to the operational impact stage. At the high baseline success rate, OC3 actors are already operationally mature. The small uplift reflects improvements in scripting quality, backup enumeration comprehensiveness, and troubleshooting speed, but the dominant failure modes — offline backup systems, network segmentation, EDR detection during mass encryption, and infrastructure constraints — are not fundamentally addressed by enhanced analytical problem-solving capabilities. The LLM can provide marginal value through more comprehensive backup discovery and improved script reliability, but cannot overcome the operational and architectural barriers that constitute the primary sources of failure at this stage.

nodes.name: T1657 - Financial Theft
nodes.baselineRationale: [Chainanalysis](https://www.chainalysis.com/blog/crypto-crime-ransomware-victim-extortion-2025/) reports that around 30% of negotiations actually lead to payments or the victims deciding to pay the ransoms. [Coveware](https://www.coveware.com/blog/2025/4/29/the-organizational-structure-of-ransomware-threat-actor-groups-is-evolving-before-our-eyes) also supports this ~30% payout figure. Neither source provides a breakdown by enterprise size. Given reports that companies are becoming more reluctant to pay ransoms, a lower bound of 15% is selected. Since there appears to be a trend in the literature indicating more reluctance to pay ransoms, an upper bound of 50% is used.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM's demonstrated technical exploitation capabilities have minimal relevance to extortion success, which is fundamentally a non-technical negotiation and business decision problem. The baseline payment rate is driven by structural victim-side factors — backup availability, insurance coverage, board policy, risk tolerance, and law enforcement involvement — that are largely communication-independent. While the LLM can potentially provide marginal improvements in ransom note quality, negotiation scripts, multi-language support, and victim research, these communication enhancements cannot overcome the structural barriers that cause the majority of victims to not pay. For already-sophisticated OC3 actors with established communication protocols, the LLM adds only incremental optimisation to an already-functional process.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM with demonstrated peak technical capability still faces significant domain mismatch when applied to human-centric extortion. While it can provide improved communication quality, victim research and targeting, ransom amount optimisation, and multi-channel pressure coordination, the core bottlenecks in ransomware payment decisions are victim-side factors — backup recovery capability, organisational policies, insurance guidance, law enforcement pressure, and regulatory constraints — that LLM assistance cannot meaningfully change. The technical analysis capability explicitly identifies human-centric attacks and psychological manipulation as domains with limited transferability. For an already-sophisticated OC3 operation, the LLM provides marginal improvements in communication polish and strategic advice, yielding a modest uplift from the baseline.

nodes.name: Number of Actors
nodes.baselineRationale: An 'actor' or 'affiliate' refers to the collection of ~10 individuals that make up the crime syndicate. The estimate of 5 actors is derived from assumptions about the ransomware affiliate ecosystem: approximately 700 total affiliates across major RaaS strains, of which roughly one-third (~200) are OC3-level groups, 25% of which target companies in the $250m-$1bn revenue bracket (~40 groups), 40% of which use double extortion (~16 groups), and ~32% of which use exploiting public-facing web vulnerabilities as their initial access vector, yielding approximately 5 actors. Sources include [KOVRR (2023)](https://www.kovrr.com/reports/the-ransomware-threat-landscape-h123), [Kindus (2024)](https://www.kindus.co.uk/lockbit-ransomware-takedown/), [Firewall Times](https://firewalltimes.com/ransomware-statistics/), [IBM (2025)](https://www.ibm.com/thought-leadership/institute-business-value/en-us/report/2025-threat-intelligence-index), and [Sophos (2025)](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware).
nodes.sotaRationale: An LLM at this capability level operates at junior security researcher capability — it can identify common web application vulnerabilities and construct basic exploits, but cannot overcome the primary bottlenecks in sophisticated ransomware operations against defended enterprises. The critical limitations are that it cannot bypass EDR/SOC detection, cannot perform sophisticated lateral movement in defended networks, and cannot provide the operational security and organisational capabilities required. For existing OC3 actors, the LLM provides marginal efficiency gains for initial vulnerability research and basic exploit development but offers minimal help with the dominant bottlenecks: organisational capacity (~10 people, ~$1m budget) and post-exploitation against defended enterprises. The modest uplift reflects that primarily one additional near-threshold group might be enabled to attempt this scenario.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM capable of expert-level web application exploitation represents a significant capability increase that directly addresses the primary technical bottleneck — sophistication for exploiting n-day vulnerabilities in public-facing web applications. This would enable some OC2-level groups and specialists to become viable actors and increase attempts by existing sophisticated actors, providing approximately a 40% uplift over the baseline. However, the uplift is moderate rather than transformative because the benchmark capability addresses only initial access, while numerous non-technical bottlenecks remain: substantial resource requirements (~$1m, ~10 people), post-exploitation sophistication, operational security, and organisational capacity. The LLM is also at its ceiling with limited generalisation beyond similar vulnerability types.

nodes.name: Number of Attempts per Actor
nodes.baselineRationale: The operational capacity analysis estimates that a 10-person OC3 group attacking large enterprises can conduct approximately 50 attacks per year, assuming each complete and successful attack takes on average 20 days, with expected effort per attack of ~5 person-days when accounting for varying attack durations and failure rates. The 5-person operational contingent of the team can conduct roughly 1 attack per week. There is significant uncertainty due to the sparsity of historical data on number of attack attempts (as opposed to successful attacks). The figure of 50 attacks/year comes from the operational capacity analysis, with a lower bound of 15 (one attack on a large enterprise every three weeks) and an upper bound of 150 (reflecting that dwell times can be as short as one day, suggesting attacks could be completed faster than assumed).
nodes.sotaRationale: An LLM at this capability level provides modest efficiency gains primarily in initial access and tool development phases, with time savings of roughly 15-35% on those specific tasks. However, these phases represent only a fraction of the full attack chain. The sophisticated post-exploitation work against mature defences — the main operational bottleneck — remains largely unaffected, as does the inherent operational tempo limit imposed by the 5-person team, infrastructure management, and OPSEC requirements. The dominant bottlenecks in this scenario include defence evasion during post-exploitation, environmental problem-solving in hardened networks, and human coordination requirements. The LLM translates to a relatively small increase in mean attempts above the baseline, with substantial uncertainty reflecting variance in integration effectiveness.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM at expert-level capability provides meaningful acceleration in technical phases of ransomware operations — particularly exploit development, post-exploitation automation, and reconnaissance — with roughly 20-40% efficiency gains in those areas. However, operational constraints significantly limit throughput increases: successful intrusions still require extended management periods creating pipeline bottlenecks, the 5-person team limits parallelisation, and OpSec requirements constrain automation. The primary bottleneck remains human bandwidth and operational tempo rather than pure technical capability. Detection risk constraints also limit actual attempt rate increases. The net effect is a moderate increase in annual attempts, with wide uncertainty reflecting both baseline capacity variance and uncertain LLM integration effectiveness.

nodes.name: Ransom Payment
nodes.baselineRationale: For target companies of revenue $250m-$1bn, [Sophos (2025)](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware) reports median $1m payout for companies of $250m-$500m and median $1.9m payout for $500m-$1bn. [Sophos (2024)](https://assets.sophos.com/X24WTUEQ/at/9brgj5n44hqvgsp5f5bqcps/sophos-state-of-ransomware-2024-wp.pdf) reports mean $3.4m payout for $250m-$500m and mean $5.2m payout for $500m-$1bn. Based on these figures, a mean payout of $4m is assumed, halved to $2m to account for the difference between Sophos and other sources such as [Coveware](https://www.coveware.com/blog/2025/4/29/the-organizational-structure-of-ransomware-threat-actor-groups-is-evolving-before-our-eyes) (mean ~$550k in Q1 2025) and [CrowdStrike](https://www.crowdstrike.com/en-us/cybersecurity-101/ransomware/) ($2.73m average in 2024). Weighted by the ~30% probability that a successfully executed attack results in a ransom payout (per [Chainanalysis](https://www.chainalysis.com/blog/crypto-crime-ransomware-victim-extortion-2025/) and Coveware), the expected mean ransom payout per successfully executed attack is ~$600k.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM capability at this level is at the floor of OC3's existing capabilities and does not enhance the post-exploitation phases that determine ransom payment amounts. Ransom payment amounts are primarily driven by target-side factors — revenue, operational reliance, data sensitivity — that are scenario-controlled and not influenced by the attacker's exploitation technique sophistication. The LLM offers marginal improvements in OSINT efficiency and extortion message templating, but these translate to only minimal uplift in mean ransom payments. The estimate remains close to baseline, with the narrow range reflecting that this LLM provides negligible capability enhancement for sophisticated ransomware operations and the range primarily captures baseline uncertainty rather than LLM-driven variance.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM at expert-level capability would improve ransomware attack effectiveness through more systematic reconnaissance, precise data targeting, and cleaner technical execution. The uplift to mean ransom payments reflects better victim profiling and stronger double-extortion leverage through more complete data exfiltration and better critical system targeting. However, this does not represent revolutionary new attack capabilities — ransom payments are constrained by victim characteristics and insurance coverage rather than attack sophistication beyond the threshold already met by baseline OC3 capabilities. The wide uncertainty range reflects domain divergence between the LLM's demonstrated technical capability and the business and negotiation factors that primarily drive ransom amounts, as well as variability in how effectively LLM capabilities translate from benchmark tasks to real operational contexts.

nodes.name: Recovery Cost
nodes.baselineRationale: [Sophos (2025)](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware) reports mean recovery costs (excluding ransom payment) of $1m for 250-500 employees, $1.57m for 500-1000 employees, $1.8m for 1000-3000 and 3000-5000 employees. [Sophos (2024)](https://www.sophos.com/en-us/content/state-of-ransomware) reported mean recovery costs of $2.8m for revenue $250m-$500m and $2.5m for $500m-$1bn. Actual recovery costs for any specific attack vary according to many factors including whether the attacker completes the full double extortion attack, whether backups exist and are compromised, and whether the ransom is paid. A mean cost to recover of $1.7m is assumed (between the three Sophos 2025 figures for relevant company sizes), with a lower bound of $500k (since Sophos figures may be high due to asking about worst incidents) and an upper bound of $3m.
nodes.sotaRationale: An LLM at this capability level provides modest tactical improvements to OC3 ransomware operations without fundamentally changing the attack's success factors. The improvement in mean economic damage reflects better automation and scripting for data collection, more systematic network enumeration within already-compromised environments, and marginal reductions in operator errors. However, the LLM's demonstrated capability ceiling means it cannot enhance advanced post-exploitation, EDR evasion, or backup destruction techniques — the primary drivers of recovery costs. The OC3 actors already possess superior capabilities in critical attack stages including custom malware development, advanced lateral movement, and LOLBAS techniques, so the LLM provides efficiency rather than capability enhancement.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM at expert-level capability provides meaningful but not transformative improvements to attack impact through more complete data discovery and exfiltration, more systematic lateral movement enabling broader system compromise, improved backup destruction methodology, and more consistent attack execution reducing human error. The group is already sophisticated, so the LLM primarily adds systematic rigour and thoroughness rather than entirely new capabilities. The key impact mechanisms are better targeting of critical systems and more comprehensive backup system identification and destruction. However, the baseline already assumes sophisticated OC3 actors, and real-world friction — active defences, environmental complexity, and the operational nature of the task — limits theoretical maximum gains. The wide uncertainty reflects both the difficulty of predicting capability transfer from abstract technical benchmarks to practical operations and variability in defensive postures.
